div.p:
Use Case:
Modify one page table entry
(map, unmap, change permissions) Latency: ~10-20 cycles
Scope: One virtual page

Use Case:
Address space switch
(context switch to new process) Latency: ~100-500 cycles
Scope: All non-global pages

text.tspan: // Flush entire TLB via CR3 reload
// Flush entire TLB via CR3 reload

div.p:
Use Case:
PCID/ASID-aware flush
(modern x86-64 with PCID) Latency: ~15-30 cycles
Scope: Pages for specific ASID

g.text: invlpg | 1 page | ~15 cycles | Single PTE update
CR3 reload | All non-global | ~200 cycles | Context switch
invlpga | ASID pages | ~25 cycles | PCID systems
Global bit | All non-global | 0 cycles | Kernel pages

ol.li: Global pages (G bit) survive CR3 reload — map kernel with G=1 to avoid re-walking page tables on every syscall.
invlpg is serializing — it forces the CPU to complete all prior memory operations before continuing.
CR3 reload on context switch is unavoidable for process isolation — each process needs its own page directory.
PCID (Process-Context Identifier) on modern CPUs allows multiple address spaces in TLB simultaneously, reducing flush frequency.
TLB shootdown on SMP: when one CPU modifies a page table, it must notify other CPUs to flush their TLB entries for those pages.
